bytes.topic: Three Pillars — logs, metrics, traces and why you need all three
bytes.title: Grafana's Three Pillars — Why Loki, Mimir, and Tempo Only Work Together
bytes.body:
Grafana's observability stack maps each pillar to a dedicated store, but they compound only when correlated.

Metrics (Prometheus/Mimir + PromQL) answer is something wrong? histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) spikes — you get an alert. Fast, cheap, but dimensionless. You know latency degraded; you don't know why.

Logs (Loki + LogQL) answer what happened? Structured filtering via | json, | pattern, and label_format lets you isolate error events by service, pod, or user. LogQL's count_over_time even bridges into metrics space — but raw logs without a trace ID are grep archaeology.

Traces (Tempo + TraceQL) answer where in the call graph? {duration > 500ms && resource.service.name = "checkout"} pinpoints slow spans across services in a single query.

Grafana's glue closes the loop: derived fields in the Loki datasource config extract trace_id from log lines and deep-link directly into Tempo's trace view. Prometheus exemplars embed trace IDs inside metric scrapes. In Grafana Explore, you pivot from a PromQL anomaly to correlated Loki logs to the exact Tempo trace — one browser tab, no context switch. That's what compresses MTTR from minutes to seconds.
bytes.code_snippet:
# Loki datasource provisioning — derived field links log trace_id → Tempo
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Loki
    type: loki
    url: http://loki:3100
    jsonData:
      derivedFields:
        - name: TraceID
          matcherRegex: 'trace_id=(\w+)'
          url: '${__value.raw}'
          datasourceUid: tempo
          urlDisplayLabel: Open in Tempo
  - name: Tempo
    type: tempo
    uid: tempo
    url: http://tempo:3200
    jsonData:
      tracesToLogsV2:
        datasourceUid: loki
        filterByTraceID: true

bytes.topic: Prometheus — metrics model, PromQL, scraping, recording rules, alerting rules
bytes.title: Grafana + Prometheus — Recording Rules, PromQL, and Alert Evaluation in Practice
bytes.body:
Prometheus stores metrics as time series identified by a metric name and key-value labels. Four types matter: counters (monotonically increasing), gauges (point-in-time values), histograms (bucketed observations), and summaries. Grafana's Prometheus data source sends PromQL at every panel refresh — expensive for high-cardinality expressions over long retention windows.

Recording rules solve this. Defined in Prometheus `rule_files`, they precompute heavy expressions into new series on a fixed interval. In Grafana dashboards, replace raw expressions like `rate(http_requests_total[5m])` with the recorded series `job:http_requests:rate5m` — panel load time drops significantly, and the series is also indexable in Explore.

Alerting rules share the same YAML group structure. When using Grafana Mimir or Cortex as your backend, Grafana's built-in ruler evaluates these rules directly — no separate Prometheus instance needed. The `for` duration prevents flapping by requiring the condition to hold continuously. Use `{{ $value | humanizePercentage }}` in `annotations` to embed live metric values in Grafana notification templates, and `labels` to control Alertmanager routing trees.
bytes.code_snippet:
groups:
  - name: http_metrics
    interval: 1m
    rules:
      - record: job:http_requests:rate5m
        expr: sum by (job) (rate(http_requests_total[5m]))

      - alert: HighErrorRate
        expr: |
          sum by (job) (rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))
          /
          sum by (job) (rate(http_requests_total[5m])) > 0.05
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "{{ $labels.job }} error rate {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"

bytes.topic: Grafana — dashboard design, data sources, alerting, variable templating
bytes.title: Grafana $__rate_interval — Fix Your PromQL Queries Across Every Zoom Level
bytes.body:
Hardcoding `[5m]` in PromQL range vectors silently breaks when users zoom into a 30-second window or pull 7 days of history. Grafana's `$__interval` macro computes the optimal step as `timeRange / maxDataPoints`, where `maxDataPoints` defaults to panel pixel width — but `$__interval` can shrink below your Prometheus scrape interval, producing "no data" gaps.

Use `$__rate_interval` instead. It's always `max(4 × scrape_interval, $__interval)`, guaranteeing enough samples for `rate()` and `increase()` regardless of zoom. Set `Min step` in the query editor (e.g., `15s`) to hard-floor the resolution without overriding `$__rate_interval`'s upper logic.

For variable-driven dashboards, combine this with a `$service` regex-match variable backed by a `label_values(up, job)` query so the interval adapts per-series automatically. In Loki metric queries, `$__interval` maps directly to the LogQL `step` parameter — `rate({app=~"$service"}[$__interval])` follows the same adaptive contract.

Avoid the `Resolution` 1/2x multiplier unless you're explicitly trading fidelity for query performance on wide ranges.

bytes.topic: OpenTelemetry — vendor-neutral instrumentation, SDK setup, collector pipeline
bytes.title: Grafana Alloy + OpenTelemetry — Wiring the LGTM Collector Pipeline
bytes.body:
OpenTelemetry's SDK-to-collector model maps directly onto Grafana's LGTM stack: traces → Tempo, metrics → Mimir, logs → Loki — all funneled through Grafana Alloy. Alloy replaces the standalone OTel Collector with a River-config pipeline of typed, explicitly wired components.

Point your SDK's OTLP HTTP exporter at Alloy's receiver on port 4318. Inside Alloy, `otelcol.receiver.otlp` ingests the signal, `otelcol.processor.batch` buffers for throughput, and outputs fan out: `otelcol.exporter.otlp` forwards traces to Tempo's gRPC endpoint (`tempo:4317`), while `otelcol.exporter.prometheus` exposes metrics for Mimir scraping. River wires these as a typed graph — `output { traces = [otelcol.processor.batch.default.input] }` — and Alloy validates the entire pipeline at startup, rejecting misconfigured exporters before a single span arrives.

Once traces land in Tempo, switch to **Grafana Explore → TraceQL**: `{ .service.name = "api" && duration > 500ms }` surfaces slow spans instantly. Enable Tempo's derived fields to auto-link trace IDs to Loki log lines — no manual correlation needed. The entire signal path stays vendor-neutral at the SDK layer; only Alloy's exporter config is Grafana-specific.
bytes.code_snippet:
ctx := context.Background()
exp, _ := otlptracehttp.New(ctx,
	otlptracehttp.WithEndpoint("http://alloy:4318"),
	otlptracehttp.WithInsecure(),
)
tp := trace.NewTracerProvider(
	trace.WithBatcher(exp),
	trace.WithResource(resource.NewWithAttributes(
		semconv.SchemaURL,
		semconv.ServiceName("my-api"),
	)),
)
otel.SetTracerProvider(tp)
defer tp.Shutdown(ctx)

bytes.topic: Distributed Tracing — spans, trace context propagation, sampling strategies
bytes.title: Grafana Tempo — Distributed Tracing with Spans, Context Propagation, and Sampling
bytes.body:
Grafana Tempo stores traces as flat, tag-based objects — no indexes, pure object storage. Each trace is a tree of spans linked by a shared `traceID` and parent `spanID`. When a request crosses service boundaries, trace context travels via W3C `traceparent` headers (or Zipkin B3); Tempo's OpenTelemetry Collector pipeline extracts these and stitches spans into a unified trace view in Grafana's Explore tab.

Sampling is where most teams bleed signal. Tempo supports both head-based sampling (decided at trace ingestion via the OTel Collector's `probabilistic_sampler`) and tail-based sampling via `tail_sampling` processor — the latter lets you keep 100% of error traces and 1% of success traces. Configure this in your Collector pipeline, not in the SDK.

Once traces land in Tempo, use TraceQL to slice them — `{ span.http.status_code = 500 && duration > 2s }` surfaces slow error spans directly. Wire Tempo as a data source in Grafana, then link it to your Loki logs and Prometheus metrics via Grafana's Correlations feature for full observability in a single pane.
bytes.code_snippet:
# otel-collector-config.yaml — tail-based sampling for Tempo
processors:
  tail_sampling:
    decision_wait: 10s
    policies:
      - name: keep-errors
        type: status_code
        status_code: { status_codes: [ERROR] }
      - name: probabilistic-success
        type: probabilistic
        probabilistic: { sampling_percentage: 1 }

exporters:
  otlp:
    endpoint: tempo:4317
    tls:
      insecure: true

service:
  pipelines:
    traces:
      receivers: [otlp]
      processors: [tail_sampling]
      exporters: [otlp]

bytes.topic: Sentry — error capture, source maps, performance monitoring, session replay
bytes.title: Grafana Faro — Sentry-Style Error Capture, Source Maps, and Session Replay Inside Your Grafana Stack
bytes.body:
Grafana Faro is the native answer to Sentry inside the Grafana stack — capturing frontend errors, Web Vitals, custom traces, and session recordings without leaving your existing Loki/Tempo pipeline. Install `@grafana/faro-web-sdk` and `@grafana/faro-web-tracing`, then call `initializeFaro()` once at app bootstrap.

`getWebInstrumentations()` automatically captures unhandled exceptions, console errors, and Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS), shipping them as structured Loki log streams tagged with `app`, `version`, and `session_id`. Add `TracingInstrumentation` and every `fetch`/`XHR` call emits an OpenTelemetry span to Tempo — distributed traces correlated to the exact frontend error that triggered them.

Source map support: upload `.map` files to Grafana Cloud's source map store (or self-hosted via the Faro collector's `sourceMaps.enabled: true` config), and stack traces in Grafana Explore de-obfuscate automatically.

Session replay uses `SessionInstrumentation` to record DOM events client-side. Replays surface in the Grafana Frontend Observability app, linked to the error event's `session_id` label in Loki — jumping from alert → trace → replay in one click without ever leaving your Grafana instance.

bytes.topic: Datadog — APM, log management, synthetics, unified platform trade-offs
bytes.title: Grafana vs Datadog — Replicating APM, Logs, and Synthetics Without the $23/Host Bill
bytes.body:
Datadog's appeal is one-pane-of-glass: APM traces link to logs, synthetics trigger alerts, and everything correlates automatically. Grafana replicates this with Tempo (traces), Loki (logs), k6 (synthetics), and Prometheus (metrics) — but correlation is opt-in, not automatic.

The critical wiring is Loki's `derivedFields`: extract a `traceId` from log lines and Grafana renders a jump-to-Tempo link inline, mirroring Datadog's APM-to-log correlation. Without this config, your stack is four disconnected tools.

Datadog's synthetic monitors use a GUI-first model with global PoPs. Grafana k6 requires you to self-host or use k6 Cloud, but scripts are pure JS, version-controllable, and CI-native — `k6 run` in GitHub Actions beats Datadog's webhook triggers for shift-left testing.

Real trade-off: Datadog's cardinality limits (custom metrics cap at 100/host on standard plans) punish high-dimensionality label sets. Grafana Mimir + Prometheus has no such hard cap — your cost scales with storage, not instrumentation decisions. For teams burning >$5k/month on Datadog, the Grafana OSS stack with Grafana Cloud's free tier for alerting is a legitimate migration path.
bytes.code_snippet:
# grafana/provisioning/datasources/loki.yaml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Loki
    type: loki
    url: http://loki:3100
    jsonData:
      derivedFields:
        - name: TraceID
          matcherRegex: 'traceId=(\w+)'
          url: '${__value.raw}'
          datasourceUid: tempo-uid  # Links directly into Tempo trace view
          urlDisplayLabel: 'Open in Tempo'

bytes.topic: Alerting — alert fatigue, actionable alerts, runbook links, severity levels
bytes.title: Grafana Alerting — Killing Alert Fatigue with Severity Labels, Runbooks, and Smart Routing
bytes.body:
Alert fatigue in Grafana usually traces back to three antipatterns: no severity labels, missing runbook links, and firing on every blip instead of sustained conditions.

Start with labels. Every alert rule should carry a `severity` label — `critical`, `warning`, or `info` — and route via Notification Policies using label matchers. Critical pages PagerDuty; warning posts to Slack; info stays in Grafana's alert history only. This routing lives in the Notification Policy tree, not scattered across individual contact points.

For `for` clauses: never fire on a single evaluation. A `for: 5m` on a P99 alert eliminates transient spikes. Pair this with `keep_firing_for` to hold the alert state while dashboards load mid-incident.

Runbook links go in the `annotations.runbook_url` field — Grafana renders this as a clickable link in both the alert detail panel and outbound notification payloads. Silence unactionable alerts via the Grafana Alerting Silences API rather than disabling rules outright, so coverage automatically resumes.

Inhibition is handled inside Notification Policies: nest a child route that matches `severity=warning` and `team=backend`, then set it to only fire when no active `critical` alert shares the same `team` label.
bytes.code_snippet:
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/api/v1/provisioning/alert-rules \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $GRAFANA_API_TOKEN" \
  -d '{
    "title": "API P99 > 2s",
    "condition": "C",
    "for": "5m",
    "keep_firing_for": "10m",
    "labels":      { "severity": "critical", "team": "backend" },
    "annotations": {
      "runbook_url": "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/api-latency",
      "summary":     "P99 latency breached 2s — check upstream DB query plans"
    }
  }'

bytes.topic: SLIs, SLOs, SLAs — defining what good looks like, error budgets
bytes.title: Grafana SLOs — Turning Raw Metrics into Error Budgets and Burn Rate Alerts
bytes.body:
Grafana's native SLO plugin (grafana-slo-app) converts a PromQL ratio into a full observability contract — SLI calculation, error budget tracking, and multi-window burn rate alerting — in one API call.

An SLI is a ratio metric: good_events / total_events expressed in PromQL. An SLO wraps it with a target and window (99.9% over 30 days). An SLA is the external commitment — typically 0.3–0.5% below your SLO to absorb incident response time.

Error budget = (1 − 0.999) × 43,200 min = 43.2 minutes/month. Grafana renders remaining budget on the auto-generated dashboard.

When you POST to the SLO API, Grafana provisions:
- Recording rules across five windows (5m, 30m, 1h, 2h, 6h)
- Multi-window burn rate alerts: fast burn (14.4× for 1h + 5m windows) and slow burn (1× for 6h + 30m windows)
- A pre-built burndown dashboard linked to the SLO

Burn rate = actual error rate ÷ error budget rate. A 14.4× rate exhausts the monthly budget in ~2 hours. The dual-window condition — both windows must breach simultaneously — is Grafana's mechanism to suppress transient spikes from paging on-call.

bytes.topic: Log Management — structured logging, log levels, correlation IDs, retention
bytes.title: Grafana Loki — Structured Logs, Correlation IDs, and Retention That Actually Works
bytes.body:
Grafana Loki ingests logs as streams identified by label sets — not indexed field values. This distinction is critical: labels like {app="api", env="prod"} determine cardinality and cost, while structured metadata lives inside the log line itself.

Use LogFmt or JSON log lines and parse them at query time with Loki's `| logfmt` or `| json` pipeline stages. For log levels, emit a `level` field in your structured payload — Loki's detected_fields will surface it automatically, and you can filter with `| level="error"`.

Correlation IDs are the real power move: inject a `trace_id` field into every log line, then link Loki datasource to Tempo in Grafana's datasource settings. Any log with a `traceID` field becomes a clickable link straight into the distributed trace — zero extra tooling.

For retention, configure per-stream policies in `loki.yaml` under `compactor.retention_enabled: true` with `stream_retention` rules. Pin high-value streams (errors, audit) to 90 days; debug streams to 7. This beats global TTLs by orders of magnitude for cost control.
bytes.code_snippet:
# loki.yaml — per-stream retention + structured log pipeline
compactor:
  retention_enabled: true

limits_config:
  retention_period: 30d
  stream_retention:
    - selector: '{level="error"}'
      period: 90d
    - selector: '{level="debug"}'
      period: 7d

# LogQL: filter by level, extract trace_id, jump to Tempo
{
  app="api",
  env="prod"
}
| json
| level="error"
| line_format "{{.trace_id}} {{.msg}}"

bytes.topic: Metrics Design — naming conventions, cardinality explosion, counter vs gauge vs histogram
bytes.title: Grafana Metrics Design — Naming, Cardinality, and Choosing the Right Type
bytes.body:
Grafana's query performance lives or dies on instrumentation quality. Name metrics using `{namespace}_{subsystem}_{name}_{unit}` — `http_server_request_duration_seconds`, not `duration`. Grafana's Explore autocomplete and variable template dropdowns rely on consistent prefixes to surface the right series.

Cardinality kills dashboards quietly. Every unique label combination creates a separate time series in Prometheus; Grafana loads all of them at panel render time. Labels like `user_id`, `trace_id`, or `path` with raw URL values balloon your TSDB and freeze Explore. Stick to bounded-cardinality labels: `status_code`, `method`, `region`.

Type selection determines your PromQL strategy in Grafana:

- **Counter**: always wrap in `rate()` or `increase()` — raw counter values are meaningless in time-series panels. Use `increase()` in Stat panels for totals over the dashboard time range.
- **Gauge**: current state (memory, queue depth) — query directly or use `min_over_time` / `max_over_time`.
- **Histogram**: the only type enabling percentile panels. `histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))` powers your p99 SLO panel. Bucket boundaries are fixed at instrument time — Grafana cannot adjust them retroactively.

bytes.topic: Dashboards — RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration), USE method for resources
bytes.title: Grafana Dashboards — RED and USE Methods Side by Side
bytes.body:
Grafana natively supports both RED (Rate, Errors, Duration) and USE (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) methods, and the key is knowing which panel belongs to which method. RED targets your services; USE targets your infrastructure resources.

For RED, wire three stat or time-series panels per service using Prometheus: `rate(http_requests_total[5m])` for rate, `rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]) / rate(http_requests_total[5m])` for error ratio, and `histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))` for p99 latency. Group these in a Grafana row per service and use `$service` template variables to fan out.

For USE, target node_exporter metrics: CPU utilization via `1 - avg(rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"}[5m]))`, saturation via `node_load1 / count(node_cpu_seconds_total{mode="idle"})`, and errors via `rate(node_disk_io_time_seconds_total[5m])`. Use Grafana's threshold bands (80%/90%) on gauge panels to make saturation immediately visible.

Combine both method rows in a single dashboard with a `datasource` variable so the same layout works across staging and production Prometheus instances without duplication.

bytes.topic: On-call — incident response, postmortems, escalation policies
bytes.title: Grafana OnCall — Wiring Escalation Chains for Real Incident Response
bytes.body:
Grafana OnCall models on-call workflows as escalation chains — ordered step sequences bound to alert routes. Each chain defines who gets paged, when, and via which channel (SMS, Slack, phone call). Steps execute sequentially with configurable wait durations between them.

The four step types you'll use most: `notify_on_call_from_schedule` (targets whoever's on shift per your rotation calendar), `notify_persons` (static fallback targets), `wait` (timeout before the next step fires), and `trigger_webhook` (bridges to external runbooks or PagerDuty escalation).

Chains bind to alert sources via Routes — regex or Alertmanager label matchers on integration payloads. A `severity=critical` label can skip the standard chain entirely and route directly to a senior engineer schedule.

For postmortems, Grafana Incident integrates natively with OnCall: attach the alert group, timeline events auto-populate from the escalation log, and the incident summary exports to Confluence or GitHub Issues out of the box.

One common gap: any `wait` step longer than your SLA breach window is a silent liability — audit your chains against your error budget targets.
bytes.code_snippet:
BASE="https://your-grafana.example.com/api/plugins/grafana-oncall-app/resources"
TOKEN="Bearer $GRAFANA_API_KEY"
CHAIN_ID="<escalation-chain-id>"

# Step 0: notify on-call schedule immediately
curl -sX POST "$BASE/escalation_policies/" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"escalation_chain_id\":\"$CHAIN_ID\",\"position\":0,\"type\":\"notify_on_call_from_schedule\",\"notify_on_call_from_schedule\":\"<schedule-id>\"}"

# Step 1: wait 5 minutes, then escalate to backup
curl -sX POST "$BASE/escalation_policies/" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"escalation_chain_id\":\"$CHAIN_ID\",\"position\":1,\"type\":\"wait\",\"duration\":300}"

curl -sX POST "$BASE/escalation_policies/" \
  -H "Authorization: $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d "{\"escalation_chain_id\":\"$CHAIN_ID\",\"position\":2,\"type\":\"notify_persons\",\"persons_to_notify\":[\"<backup-user-id>\"]}"

bytes.topic: Cost of Observability — high cardinality metrics are expensive, sampling trade-offs
bytes.title: Grafana — High Cardinality Metrics Will Burn Your Budget (And How to Stop Them)
bytes.body:
Every unique label-value combination in Prometheus/Mimir creates a distinct time series in TSDB. Add a `user_id` label to a request counter and you've multiplied your series count by your entire user base — Grafana Mimir starts rejecting writes once you breach `max_global_series_per_user` (default: 150k per tenant).

The cost is concrete: Grafana Cloud bills per active series, and TSDB head compaction degrades linearly with cardinality. Audit your exposure in Grafana Explore with `grafana_tsdb_head_series` or use Mimir's `/api/v1/cardinality/label_names` endpoint to identify the worst offenders.

Fix it at ingestion, not in dashboards. Grafana Alloy's `prometheus.relabel` component lets you strip or truncate unbounded labels before they hit remote_write. For traces, Grafana Tempo's tail-based sampler is the right lever: configure `tail_sampling` policies to keep 100% of error/slow spans while dropping 90%+ of healthy ones — far more cost-efficient than flat-rate head sampling at 10%. Instrument broadly; filter aggressively at the collector edge.

bytes.topic: Synthetic Monitoring — uptime checks, multi-step API tests, SLA alerting
bytes.title: Grafana Synthetic Monitoring — Uptime Checks, Multi-Step API Tests, and SLA Alerting
bytes.body: Grafana Synthetic Monitoring runs checks from global probes and exposes results as Prometheus metrics you can alert on directly. Basic HTTP checks hit an endpoint and track `probe_success` and `probe_duration_seconds`. For multi-step API flows — auth → fetch → assert — use Scripted checks backed by k6. These run your k6 script from selected probe locations on a configurable interval (minimum 10s). Results land in your Grafana Cloud metrics store under the `synthetic_monitoring` namespace. For SLA alerting, create a Grafana Alerting rule querying `avg_over_time(probe_success{job="my-api"}[30d]) < 0.999` to fire when 30-day availability drops below 99.9%. Pair this with a `probe_duration_seconds{quantile="0.95"}` rule to catch latency degradation before users feel it. Checks are managed via the Synthetic Monitoring API or Terraform (`grafana_synthetic_monitoring_check` resource), so probe config lives in version control alongside your dashboards-as-code.

bytes.topic: Profiling — continuous profiling, flame graphs, finding hotspots in production
bytes.title: Grafana Pyroscope — Continuous Profiling and Flame Graph Diff in Production
bytes.body:
Grafana Pyroscope gives you always-on continuous profiling without the overhead of one-shot tools. Pyroscope scrapes pprof-compatible endpoints every 15s by default, storing profiles as compressed call-tree blocks queryable via ProfileQL.

In Grafana Explore > Profiles, pick a service_name and profile_type (e.g. process_cpu:cpu:nanoseconds:cpu:nanoseconds). The native flame graph panel — GA since Grafana 9.5 — renders call stacks width-proportional to CPU time. Wider frame = hotter path. Click any frame to scope the view; right-click to copy the symbol for a targeted ProfileQL query.

For production hotspot hunting, use Comparison Mode: set the left window to your degraded time range and the right to a healthy baseline. Grafana renders a delta flame graph — red frames regressed, blue improved — so you see exactly which call paths changed, not just that something got slower.

Pin label selectors like {service_name="checkout-api", pod=~"checkout-.*"} to isolate pod-level variance in Kubernetes deployments. Profiles are also linkable from Grafana Tempo traces via trace-to-profiles correlation, giving you the full latency → trace → hotspot path in one click.

bytes.topic: Health Checks — liveness, readiness, dependency health, uptime monitoring
bytes.title: Grafana Health Checks — Liveness, Readiness, and Datasource Dependency Monitoring
bytes.body:
Grafana exposes two native health endpoints out of the box. `GET /api/health` returns liveness state — JSON with `database`, `version`, and `commit`. If `database` is anything other than `"ok"`, Grafana is up but degraded. For readiness, pair this with `/healthz`, which returns HTTP 200 only when Grafana is fully initialized and ready to serve traffic — useful as a Kubernetes readiness probe.

For dependency health, use `GET /api/datasources/:id/health` (introduced in Grafana 9.0). This triggers each datasource's own health check — Prometheus scrapes, Loki connectivity, Postgres ping — and returns `status: ok | error` with a message. Automate this across all datasources via the Search API (`GET /api/datasources`) to build a dependency health sweep.

For uptime monitoring, scrape Grafana's built-in Prometheus metrics at `/metrics`. Track `grafana_datasource_request_duration_seconds` and `grafana_api_response_status_total` to alert on datasource latency regressions before users notice broken panels. Wire these into an alerting rule with a `for: 2m` hold to suppress transient blips.
bytes.code_snippet:
#!/bin/bash
GRAFANA_URL="http://localhost:3000"
AUTH="admin:admin"

# Liveness
curl -sf "$GRAFANA_URL/api/health" | jq '.database'

# Readiness
curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "$GRAFANA_URL/healthz"

# Datasource dependency health sweep
curl -su "$AUTH" "$GRAFANA_URL/api/datasources" | \
  jq '.[].id' | \
  while read id; do
    echo "DS $id:"
    curl -su "$AUTH" "$GRAFANA_URL/api/datasources/$id/health" | jq '{status,message}'
  done

bytes.topic: When to Use What — Prometheus+Grafana vs Datadog vs New Relic decision framework
bytes.title: Grafana + Prometheus vs Datadog vs New Relic — A Decision Framework for Observability Stacks
bytes.body:
If you're already running Grafana, the real question is whether to pair it with Prometheus (self-hosted) or route to a managed backend like Datadog or New Relic.

Choose Prometheus+Grafana when: you need full data ownership, your team can operate alertmanager and Thanos/Cortex for long-term storage, and cost at scale matters. Grafana's native Prometheus data source lets you write PromQL directly in panels and alerts — no abstraction layer. You control retention, cardinality, and scrape intervals.

Choose Datadog when: you need out-of-the-box APM with distributed tracing correlated to metrics without wiring OpenTelemetry yourself. Grafana does support Datadog as a data source, so you can still centralize dashboards — but you're paying Datadog's per-host pricing.

Choose New Relic when: your team wants NRQL-driven querying and Grafana's New Relic data source plugin is acceptable. Works well if you're already in the New Relic ecosystem for browser monitoring.

The Grafana-native path with Prometheus wins on cost and flexibility past ~50 hosts. Below that, managed platforms reduce ops burden significantly. Use Grafana's data source mixing to run a hybrid during migration — query Prometheus and Datadog side-by-side in a single dashboard to validate parity before cutover.
bytes.code_snippet:
# grafana/provisioning/datasources/mixed.yaml
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Prometheus
    type: prometheus
    url: http://prometheus:9090
    isDefault: true
  - name: Datadog
    type: grafana-datadog-datasource
    jsonData:
      apiUrl: https://api.datadoghq.com
    secureJsonData:
      apiKey: ${DD_API_KEY}
      appKey: ${DD_APP_KEY}
